119.body: Codex 0.127 — fixes for the file-watcher race that was eating PRs in tight loops. Worth the upgrade.
Codex still wins on a single-file refactor; Claude Code wins on cross-file. Both are converging anyway.

children.body: Same observation. We use Codex for the inner loop and Claude Code for the planning loop. Different tools, same toolbelt.

119.body: Or use neither and just type.

children.body: Could you say more about the release-watch part? That's the bit I'm stuck on.

122.body: Persistent memory is the wrong frame. What you actually want is *retrievable structure* — a knowledge graph you can re-derive cheaply. Most 'memory' systems I've seen are append-only logs that decay into noise around the 6-month mark.
mem0 + a Postgres + pgvector backstop. Skip the framework, run the SQL.
Two layers worked for us: a hot working set that lives in the prompt, and a cold long-term store you query through a tool. Trying to merge them into one thing was where we got into trouble.

children.body: Same shape here. The tricky part is the eviction policy from hot to cold. We tried recency, then frequency, then a hybrid; none of them are obviously right.

122.body: Most of these systems are solving a problem the user shouldn't have. If your agent needs persistent memory across sessions, the better fix is shorter, more focused sessions.

123.body: The numbers don't match what we're seeing. Curious what their workload actually looks like.
Half-right. The other half is observability — and almost no one does it.

127.body: First one of these I've seen that doesn't oversell. Appreciate the honesty about failure modes.

children.body: Right, and the corollary is that the architecture choice cascades into the eval strategy, which is rarely discussed in the same breath.

131.body: Thanks for posting. This kind of write-up is exactly why I lurk here.

132.body: This is the bundling-versus-platform tension playing out in real time. Anthropic wants Claude Code to be a closed loop; OpenClaw was always going to be friction for them.
Bad look. The community plugin ecosystem is most of why Claude Code felt different from Codex. Killing it asymmetrically is the kind of move that gets remembered.

children.body: And yet they'll get away with it. There's no plausible alternative for power users right now. That's a moat, not a mistake.

132.body: Charitable read: OpenClaw's traffic patterns broke their rate-limit assumptions and they couldn't tier-price their way out of it. The cleaner fix would have been better rate limits, not a bundle ban.

134.body: model launches has been on my list. Thanks.
Saved. Pointing our junior team at this on Monday.

135.body: Mythos is the first system card I've seen where the 'failure modes' section is longer than the 'capabilities' section. That's a healthy direction.

The agentic eval table on page 22 is the bit nobody is reading carefully. Mythos's tool-call accuracy is a regression on edge cases — 1.8% vs 1.1% for Opus 4.7. Small numbers, big consequences in production.

children.body: Yep. Mythos is being marketed as 'better' but it's a different point on the cost/quality curve, not a uniform improvement. Which is fine — just label it.

135.body: The training-data section has more detail than usual. The deduplication methodology is genuinely novel; worth reading even if you don't care about the model itself.

136.body: Good piece. The MCP angle is what most write-ups miss.

154.body: MCP is the silent killer of agent budgets. This is a clean breakdown.
Learned it the hard way last quarter. Pay the cost up front.

156.body: The frame is right but the conclusion is too clean. A2A and MCP are both early; the boundary between them is going to move at least twice before it settles.

Worth pairing with the 'one big agent vs many small' thread from a few weeks back — same shape, different layer of the abstraction. The protocols are converging on the question 'what's the right granularity for delegation', they just disagree on the answer.

children.body: Right. The interesting layer isn't tool-vs-agent, it's stateful-vs-stateless delegation. Both protocols hand-wave that part.

157.body: 3 weeks for a working product is the new normal. The interesting question isn't 'can you ship fast' anymore; it's 'can you keep shipping after the AI scaffolding stops being load-bearing'.
Or: don't. The world has enough social media tools. Build something that needed to exist.

167.body: Crowded space — Cursor, Zed, Continue, now Baton. The differentiator here is the multi-agent UI. Whether that's a feature or a sign-of-the-times depends on who you ask.
Multi-agent UI is the right pitch for 2026 but the execution risk is high. Most teams I know would rather have one excellent agent than three mediocre ones running in parallel.

168.body: Notable: the system prompt is meaningfully shorter than 4.6's. Looks like they moved a lot of behavior into post-training instead of prompt-time instruction.
Which would explain the smaller variance window I saw on tool calls. Tighter weights, fewer prompt-driven branches.

children.body: If true, it's both a quality and a cost win. Shorter prompt means more usable context for the user.

168.body: Or it's a partial leak. 'Leaked system prompts' have a 50% truthiness rate historically.

169.body: First one of these I've seen that doesn't oversell. Appreciate the honesty about failure modes.

175.body: The fact that this works is more interesting than how well it plays. MCP as a game-state interface is exactly the kind of thing nobody designed for and yet here we are.
Latency budget of an MCP round-trip is ~80ms p50 in our tests. DOOM-playable, barely. Counter-Strike, no.

children.body: p99 is what'll get you. p50 of 80ms is fine; p99 of 800ms means you eat a rocket every fight.

175.body: Repo's clean. Useful as a stress test for MCP server scaffolds.

176.body: Good piece. The Claude Code angle is what most write-ups miss.
I keep going back and forth on this. The framing is correct in steady state but breaks down during ramp-up — and ramp-up is where most of us live.

178.body: Models trained on coding data over-index on coding feel. 'Horrible at writing' is a strong claim though — I've gotten fine prose out of 4.7 with the right system prompt.
Probably true that 4.7 is worse than 4.6 on creative writing. The post-training mix shifted toward agent/tool tasks. Tradeoff visible if you measure it.

children.body: Right. Each release is a point on a multi-dimensional Pareto frontier. 'Better' depends on which axis you weight.

179.body: Going to try this on our pipeline next week. Will report back.

181.body: The pitch — Claude Code and Codex as one system — is the right pitch. The implementation is heavier than I'd want; the harness is doing a lot of work that should live in the agent loop itself.
Tried it on a sample task. Got working PR in 12 minutes with no human intervention. That's not normal yet.

children.body: Eval suite of one is impressive but unreliable. Run it on 50 tasks and tell me what the success rate looks like.

181.body: Or just use Claude Code with a tighter spec. Most 'multi-agent' setups are working around prompt deficiencies in the inner loop.

children.body: Disagree — eval coverage on game-like loops is genuinely useful. You learn things about planning horizon and recovery from bad states that don't show up in static benchmarks.
Right. There's a small but interesting literature on Atari-style envs as agent evals. Tetris is on the simpler end but the loss-condition recovery is non-trivial.

187.body: Code's clean. The MCP server abstraction over the game state is reusable for other text-grid games.
Score curve over 1000 games would be useful. Otherwise it's a demo, not an eval.

192.body: The 'is X nerfed?' thread is now a weekly tradition. 9 times out of 10 it's a context-window or system-prompt change, not a model regression.

children.body: 10/10 actually. Anthropic's been transparent about model weights being frozen post-launch. The 'nerf' is almost always your prompt drifting under you.
Concur. We A/B'd the same eval suite against the same model snapshot a week apart and got identical scores within noise. The perception is real, the regression isn't.

192.body: What did change is the rate limit tier on Pro. If you're hitting backoff more aggressively, the model feels slower even though it isn't.
Worth distinguishing 'feels worse' from 'measurably worse'. The first is real and matters; the second is rare.

193.body: Honest answer: no direct competitor at 4.6's tier on long-horizon agentic tasks. Sonnet 4.6 is close on cost-perf but loses on multi-step reasoning. GPT-5.x is competitive on single-turn but degrades faster across tool calls.

children.body: Add: Gemini 2.6 closes some of the gap on raw reasoning but the tool-calling reliability is meaningfully worse. We measured ~7% hallucinated tool args vs ~2% for Opus.

193.body: On long context specifically, nothing else is close. Opus 4.6 (and now 4.7) is in a category of one for >256k retrieval workloads.
If you're optimizing for cost-per-quality and not raw quality, Sonnet 4.6 + a planner-worker split gets you 80% of Opus performance at 30% of the cost. Different question, different answer.
Or rephrase the question: what task are you trying to do? Most teams asking this don't need Opus, they're just used to it.

278.body: 14x cost increase 'for identical work' is a strong claim. The most likely cause: the 'high-thinking' tier is now expanding internal scratchpad tokens by ~10x. Bug or feature, depending on who you ask.
We saw this too. The fix was downgrading to medium-thinking, which produced indistinguishable output for our workload. Test before you upgrade.

children.body: Right. 'Higher thinking' is not a free upgrade — it's a cost-quality dial, and most production tasks don't need the high end.

278.body: Cache hit rate is also a factor. If your prompt prefix changed at all between runs, the per-call cost can blow up by an order of magnitude.

296.body: Took me three reads, but the Claude Code bit clarified something I'd been stuck on for weeks.

297.body: Saved. Pointing our junior team at this on Monday.

children.body: Counterpoint: the planner-worker pattern, with a 200-line orchestrator.

297.body: There's a deeper question here about why Claude Code keeps re-appearing. My theory: the abstraction is leaky in a way that isn't obvious until you scale past one team.

children.body: Adding to this — the same pattern shows up in the eval harness write-up but with different second-order effects, which is why people get confused when comparing notes across teams.

298.body: The 1M context window is the headline but the recall curve is the news. Anthropic published a chart showing degradation past 600k that's much shallower than 4.6 was. Big deal for long-running agents.
Quick eval pass: SWE-bench-Verified scores up ~6 points over 4.6, but the variance across runs widened. Worth waiting a week for the field to stabilize before drawing conclusions.

children.body: Variance is the bigger story for me. A model that beats 4.6 on the median but loses on the p10 is a regression for production agents that depend on tail behavior.
The system card's eval section is unusually detailed this round. Tail behavior gets a paragraph instead of a footnote. Read past page 14.

298.body: The price hint in the API docs is the thing nobody's talking about. Per 1M output tokens at the 1M-context tier is a meaningful step up from Sonnet — not a like-for-like upgrade.
Cache hit improvements are quiet but real. Same workflow as last week, my hit rate jumped from 72% to 84% without any code change.

299.body: Anthropic is moving up the stack. Whether this is a moat or a distraction depends on what shipping velocity looks like in six months.
First impressions: the design tokens model is sane, the live preview is faster than v0, and the Figma export actually round-trips. Worth a serious look.

children.body: The Figma round-trip is what closes the gap for me — that was the missing piece in every prior tool I tried.

299.body: Tried it for an internal admin app. Output is good, but the pricing tier is going to matter — design loops eat tokens fast and the cost wasn't visible during preview.

300.body: Worth pairing with the the long-running-agent cost piece from a few weeks ago discussion from a few weeks back. They're not contradictory but they optimize for different things.

301.body: Or: don't. We tried this in production and the second-order effects ate the savings.

children.body: This matches our data too.

302.body: Solid overview. The hands-free demo at 18:30 is the bit worth jumping to.
Or read the changelog. 90 minutes for what could be a five-minute scan.
